narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your bar exam odds estimated at a 70% chance of failure feels like a gut punch, especially with your solid 3.5 undergraduate GPA and upper-middle family background. The prediction hinged most heavily on your Year 3 decile ranking of 1.0, which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward challenges ahead, suggesting your performance in those later crucial years didn't climb into higher tiers at school. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 1.0, reinforcing a pattern that the model saw as a steady hurdle from the start. Your LSAT score of 39.0 added to this picture, tipping the scales further toward difficulty in mastering the intense bar material. Even factors like your white racial background and male gender, which sometimes correlate with certain outcomes in these predictions, nudged it in that direction, alongside your choice to go full-time without other balancing strengths. Your family income in the upper-middle bracket didn't provide the lift it might for others, and while your UGPA is respectable, it couldn't offset the dominant weight of those early and late deciles. Together, these painted a story of consistent underperformance in competitive academic deciles, despite your dedication to full-time study, leading the model to see higher risks for the bar. This isn't about your potential or heart—it's a data-driven snapshot that schools use to manage their outcomes. You might consider retaking the LSAT or building a stronger late-grade trajectory to shift this narrative next time. Keep pushing; stories like yours often find a way to turn the page.
